pages.text: epic THE WILD LIFE HUMMINGBIRD FOR A DAY WRITTEN BY Whitney Matheson ILLUSTRATED BY Anna Maria Jung The Wild Life Hummingbird for a Day
HUMMMMMMM 2 Hi friend Hear that sound That’s how we hummingbirds got our name our fast flapping wings hum all day long Violetear Hummingbird

Hummingbirds are fun colorful creatures that like to be on the move How would it feel to be a hummingbird for a day Spread your wings and find out My name is Busy and I’ll be your guide Come on let’s fly

Before you soar you need to decide Which kind of hummingbird are you So Many Hummingbirds There are more than 330 species of hummingbirds They live in North America and South America and many of them migrate south in the winter RUBY THROATED HUMMINGBIRD This short legged hummingbird is the only species regularly spotted in most of the eastern United States ANNA'S HUMMINGBIRD Named for an Italian duchess it has feathers that are bright and iridescent which means that when light hits them at a certain angle they appear to change color BEE HUMMINGBIRD Not just the smallest hummingbird but actually the smallest bird in the world it's about two inches long weighs less than a dime and lives only in Cuba WIRE CRESTED THORNTAIL With a long pointed blue tail and tall feathers that look like a wild hairstyle on top of its head this small hummingbird stands out in a crowd RUFOUS HUMMINGBIRD This fast and feisty flier will fight any bird that competes with it for food It can migrate huge distances up to 4,000 miles

MARVELOUS SPATULETAIL This rare hummingbird has a long and unique tail made up of four feathers two of which look like cooking spatulas BLUE THROATED HILLSTAR Discovered in 2017 in the Andes Mountains of Ecuador this beautiful blue bird can survive in cold temperatures unlike most other species GIANT HUMMINGBIRD This is the largest and slowest flying hummingbird At about eight inches long it's the size of a cardinal and has dull green or brown feathers CALLIOPE HUMMINGBIRD Named for the ancient Greek muse of epic poetry this itty bitty hummingbird has a short bill and tail and is the smallest bird in the United States and Canada Ecuador has about 130 hummingbird species Why so many Hummingbirds thrive in places with biodiversity or many kinds of climates and habitats This South American country has warm tropical rain forests cool coastal areas and the tall Andes Mountains Many hummingbird species are endangered which means only a few of the birds remain in the wild and these species are at risk of becoming extinct or disappearing forever

Six AM WAKE UP AND GO Stay still Not you There's too much to do You beat your wings 50 to 80 times per second You're the only bird that can fly backward Other birds move their wings up and down but your wings move in a figure eight pattern This lets you hover in midair You're super speedy You can fly more than 30 miles per hour which is as fast as a car You can dive even faster You fly fast but you can't hop or walk because your legs are too weak You use your small feet to scoot from side to side Rufous breasted Hermit I'm just winging it

7 Copper rumped Hummingbird I've got the moves DIFFERENT BEATS The giant hummingbird's wings beat only 10 to 15 times per second but the wings of a tiny amethyst woodstar hummingbird beat up to 80 times per second

Eight AM ALWAYS HUNGRY Time for breakfast Or is this your second breakfast or third Three meals a day aren't nearly enough You must eat all day long to have the energy to keep flying Flowers are your main food source so you visit hundreds of them every day You like bright red orange and pink flowers best though flowers of any color will do As you hover over a flower to drink its nectar your wings beat so fast that they make a humming sound You drink more than your body weight in nectar every day For a kid that would be like eating about 130 grilled cheese sandwiches in a day Your tongue is forked When you dip it inside a flower it splits in two which helps you get even more nectar You can extend your tongue too Tiny rough hairlike fringes on your tongue called lamellae help trap the nectar as you sip it letting you gulp down as much as possible Nectar is sweet and has a healthy kind of sugar that gives you energy to fly You know how much sugar is in the nectar you drink from each flower If a flower's too low in sugar you won't feed on it

9 WHAT IS POLLINATION You are important to the pollination process which helps plants make seeds and multiply When you dip into a flower it covers your head and bill with yellow pollen Then when you visit another flower you transfer that pollen to it The pollen fertilizes cells in the flowers to make seeds which create more flowers You pollinate thousands of different wildflowers and plants keeping them alive and beautiful and giving living things more food to eat Copper rumped Hummingbird EAT LIKE A HUMMINGBIRD One Hover above a flower that has a lot of nectar Two Place your bill inside the flower Three Open your bill and extend your long forked tongue Four Extend your lamellae to lap up the nectar Five Put your tongue back in your mouth and absorb the liquid six Repeat hundreds of times each day

Ten AM Your bill is very long and perfectly shaped to dip into your favorite flowers Your thin forked tongue lets you lap up sweet liquid from flowers and feeders Your lungs take about 250 breaths per minute Humans take only 12 to 20 Your heart beats up to 1,200 times a minute during flight A human heart beats only 60 to 100 times a minute Your pectoral or chest muscles help you fly and make up more than one quarter of your body weight Long billed Starthroat Hummingbird A CLOSER LOOK Your tiny body can do incredible things Your eyes can see farther than a human's can and you can see ultraviolet light

You have tiny feet that make it impossible to walk but their size is perfect for fast flying If you are male the feathers on your throat called a gorget may be brightly colored and iridescent depending on your species You're a lightweight You weigh less than a marshmallow Female hummingbirds are larger than males Lookin good
Twelve PM My brain is small but my thoughts are HUGE Black chinned Hummingbird

TIME TO LEARN You're a gold star student with a great memory Your brain is about the size of a grain of rice You have a large hippocampus which is the part of the brain that focuses on memory and learning You remember everything including where humans place hummingbird feeders in their backyards You even recognize the humans who refill them You can recall every flower you've visited and you know how long it will take before they're full of nectar again You migrate thousands of miles each year and remember your route You even stop for food at the same places
14 Two PM Rivolis Hummingbirds Why does a hummingbird hum

15 AFTERNOON RECESS You and most other hummingbirds aren't very social You fly alone eat alone and build a nest alone But when you're with other hummingbirds you certainly do express yourself A group of hummingbirds can be called a charm a shimmer a bouquet a hover or a glittering You communicate by flying You may do a fast dive fly in a U shape or dart around to either attract another hummingbird or signal that they should get out of your way You can chirp twitter and sing Different hummingbirds make different vocal sounds You protect your territory If someone dares to get too close to your food or home you'll make noise puff out your chest raise your feathers and chase them away If you feel threatened you might even attack them with your bill Fiery throated Hummingbird Because it doesn't know the words Rivoli's Hummingbird

Four PM CLEAN THOSE FEATHERS Bath time keeps you looking your best and brightest You use your bill and feet to clean yourself throughout the day A special gland on your tail produces oil that you use to shine your feathers You use your feet like a comb and to scratch annoying itches You enjoy taking baths in streams or backyard birdbaths but if there's no tub available you wash yourself with wet leaves You like to sunbathe and will spread your feathers to dry off and catch a few warm rays WHY DO YOUR FEATHERS SHIMMER Depending on your species you may have colorful feathers that are iridescent in the sunlight That's because your feathers are made of special cells that contain melanosomes or tiny structures filled with air bubbles When sunlight reflects off these bubbles it creates stunning colors It's kind of like seeing different colors in a soap bubble but better
Please pass the shampoo Anna's Hummingbird

six PM STILL HUNGRY Sometimes nectar from flowers isn't enough to fill your tummy Nectar gives you energy but you get more protein vitamins and other nutrients from eating small insects and spiders Can't find any flowers or bugs You'll also snack on tree sap You have been known to eat sand soil and ash for their minerals Your speed and precision make you a super hunter You can use your bill to grab insects out of midair and pluck them from spiderwebs You're helpful to humans because you munch on insect pests like mosquitoes fruit flies mites aphids gnats and wasps You can consume anywhere from one to three times your body weight every day While you're eating you have to avoid predators or creatures that prey upon hummingbirds including snakes lizards bats hawks crows squirrels and toucans Broad tailed Hummingbird What does a predator call a hummingbird

Fast food BUSY'S YUMMY HUMMINGBIRD FOOD You and your friends love when humans put hummingbird feeders in their yards Here's an easy way to make hummingbird food one Combine four parts water and one part white sugar in a pitcher or bowl So if you use onebyfour cup of sugar use one cup of water Stir until the sugar is all dissolved two Pour the mixture into a hummingbird feeder three Wait for us to fly by for a yummy meal Hummingbird food should NOT include red dye or food coloring It's not healthy and can actually be harmful Besides we love clear sugar water

GUTE NACHT eight PM BEDTIME STORY Believe it or not your relatives have been humming around North Central and South America for about 22 million years The oldest hummingbird fossil was found in Germany which is strange because you're only known to live in the Americas You are related to small birds called swifts Over time some hummingbird species have evolved to match the flowers they visit For example the buff tailed sicklebill hummingbird has a unique curved bill that matches the curve of its favorite flowers Early European explorers such as Christopher Columbus and Jean de Léry were amazed by hummingbirds when they first visited the Americas They wondered Were these creatures insects or were they birds Scientists still have a lot to learn about you and new species are still being discovered Today the Andes Mountains in South America are home to 40 percent of hummingbird species
I get my good looks from my great great great grandparents COUSIN Velvet purple Coronet Hummingbird
ten PM I need my beauty rest Violetear Hummingbird

CLOSE YOUR EYES When darkness falls you finally stop moving On chilly nights you go into a deep sleep called torpor Torpor can last between five and ten hours Torpor is similar to hibernation it helps you survive cold temperatures Unlike humans you need to constantly refill your energy when you're awake Torpor slows your metabolism the process that turns food into energy This lets you go a long time without eating Your heart rate can slow to as few as 50 beats per minute while you sleep During the day it can reach 1,200 beats per minute You like to sleep on a tree branch where you feel safe from predators If you lay eggs you'll sleep in your nest More about nests on the next page Sometimes you sleep upside down What's the first thing you do after you wake from torpor Eat of course

Twelve AM WELCOME HOME You never forget your first nest Your nests are tiny It depends on your species but most hummingbird nests are small cups about the size of a golf ball When it comes to building nests female hummingbirds are in complete control Grown male hummingbirds do not build visit or even sleep in nests Nests are often built on branches more than ten feet off the ground in places safe from bad weather and predators No branch nearby Hummingbird nests can also be found inside lamps on wires or in any other spot that looks safe to a mama hummingbird Your nest is made of leaves twigs fibers lichens moss and other natural materials It is held together with stretchy spider silk It takes you a few days to build a nest Your nest is fragile and probably won't last more than one breeding season But you might rebuild it in the same spot every year You're handy at home If your nest is damaged you will try to repair it UNDER CONSTRUCTION
Welcome And don't forget to wipe your feet before you come in Nest Sweet Nest
C B A TWEEET Two AM Mom what's for breakfast

SHHH DON'T WAKE THE BABIES You grow up so fast But here's what happened after you were born Female hummingbirds usually lay two white eggs about the size of small jelly beans Your mom sat on her eggs for two or three weeks before they hatched You probably hatched in late spring or early summer depending on where you live Your mom is an amazing architect When building your nest she used spider silk the strong elastic fiber spiders produce to catch prey The silk stretched as you grew so your home was always the perfect size Baby hummingbirds are called chicks hatchlings or nestlings You didn't open your eyes or start growing any feathers until about nine days after you hatched You stayed pretty quiet as a baby except when you were really hungry When you were small your mom fed you small bugs and nectar from her bill You spent about three weeks in your mom's nest before you were big enough to fly and live on your own Every year you like to return to the same place you were born

28 Four AM LATE NIGHT MIGRATION Pack a tiny suitcase it's time to move You don't usually fly at night except during migration Migration is when you and your friends fly to somewhere warmer for a few months to avoid a cold winter You may migrate hundreds or even thousands of miles You usually migrate in late summer or early fall Before migrating you need to eat a lot to increase your body weight Your body stores a layer of fat to help you survive the long journey Most hummingbirds in Canada and the United States migrate south to Mexico or Central America Some South American species migrate north to these areas too The calliope hummingbird is the smallest migratory bird in the world The rufous hummingbird migrates the longest distance You migrate by yourself not in a flock as other birds do Even young hummingbirds go it alone when migrating for the first time You'll probably use the same migration route every year of your life Not all hummingbirds migrate If it doesn't get very cold where you live you can stay put for the winter Mexico Central America
Am I there yet Time to stop for a snack

30 six AM Birdie's DINER 24 HOURS A BEAUTIFUL MORNING No matter your species you and humans can help keep each other humming along It's important to protect your habitat which is threatened by many things including humans moving into the area trees being cut down and climate change If you recognize and are comfortable around a particular human you might eat from their hand or even perch on their finger You help humans by being an amazing pollinator spreading pollen to plants that grow delicious fruits and vegetables

It's a new day Ruby throated Hummingbird HOW CAN HUMANS HELP HUMMINGBIRDS Make sure our feeders are always clean and filled with fresh food see page 19 Create safe spaces where we can build nests Make sure they're close to food and offer protection from predators outdoor cats can pounce on us and bad weather Plant hummingbird gardens Research and plant the kinds of local native flowers hummingbirds like to snack on Support conservation groups Join local bird clubs and organizations Turn down the volume We get scared by loud noises

HUMMINGBIRD HALL OF FAME OLDEST HUMMINGBIRD 12 YEARS This broad tailed hummingbird lived more than twice as long as expected Most live for only three to five years LONGEST BILL four INCHES The sword billed hummingbird's bill is longer than its entire body FASTEST WINGS 80 BEATS PER SECOND That's how fast the amethyst woodstar hummingbird's wings flap FASTEST HEARTBEAT UP TO 1,260 BEATS PER MINUTE The blue throated mountain gem wins for speediest heartbeat A human heart clocks in at 60 to 100 beats per minute

33 FOUND MAX SPEED WACKIEST HAIR BRIGHT ORANGE MOHAWK It's sported by the rainbow bearded thornbill which also has a multicolor gorget or beard FASTEST DIVE 385 BODY LENGTHS PER SECOND OR ABOUT 50 MILES PER HOUR The Anna's hummingbird is the fastest diver on Earth relative to its size SMALLEST EGG THE SIZE OF A STAPLE The vervain hummingbird holds a world record for producing the tiniest eggs of any bird LONGEST TIME MISSING 69 YEARS The blue bearded helmetcrest was spotted in 1946 then wasn't seen again until 2015
Bye bye What an exciting day It's time for me to move on but be sure to fly back and visit soon
